title: Tic-Tac-Toe
tagline: When both players play randomly, who wins — and how often is it a draw?
overview: Tic-Tac-Toe (Noughts and Crosses) is played on an N×N grid. Two players alternately mark empty squares with their symbol (X goes first, O goes second). The first player to place N consecutive marks in a row, column, or diagonal wins. On a standard 3×3 board with two perfect players, every game ends in a draw. But with random play, the first-mover advantage becomes visible.
rules: Players alternate placing their mark (X always goes first).
Marks can be placed on any empty square.
The first player to align N marks in a row, column, or diagonal wins.
If all squares are filled with no winner, the game ends in a draw.
On a 3×3 board, a win requires 3 in a row. On 4×4 and 5×5 boards, you need N in a row.
Random play means each player picks uniformly at random from all available empty squares.
monteCarloExplanation: Both players select moves uniformly at random from available empty squares. This removes any strategic bias, so the results purely reflect structural advantages: going first (X) has a natural edge because there are more positions where X can win. Larger boards tend to produce more draws as the game space expands. The simulation estimates outcome probabilities and average game length across thousands of games.

strategies.name: Random X (First Player)
strategies.description: Selects any empty square uniformly at random. Benefits from going first.

strategies.name: Random O (Second Player)
strategies.description: Selects any empty square uniformly at random. Must overcome the first-mover disadvantage.
